revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:بيودو ان]][[bg:Бьодо-ин]][[cs:Bjódóin]][[de:Byōdō-in]][[el:Μπιόντο-ιν]][[en:Byōdō-in]][[es:Byōdō-in]][[eu:Byōdō-in]][[fa:بیودو-این]][[fi:Byōdō-in]][[fr:Byōdō-in]][[gan:平等院]][[hr:Byodo-in]][[hu:Bjódóin]][[id:Byōdō-in]][[it:Byōdō-in]][[ja:平等院]][[ko:뵤도인]][[la:Byōdō-in]][[li:Byōdō-in]][[ms:Byōdō-in]][[nl:Byodo-in]][[pl:Byōdō-in]][[pt:Byōdō-in]][[ro:Byodo-In]][[ru:Бёдо-ин]][[sh:Byōdō-in]][[simple:Byōdō-in]][[sl:Bjodo-in]][[th:เบียวโดอิง]][[tr:Byōdō-in]][[uk:Монастир Бьодо]][[vi:Byōdō-in]][[zh-yue:平等院]][[zh:平等院]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Phoenix Hall, Byodo-in, November 2016 -01.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Buddhism|qid=Q748|1=Buddhism}}; {{ill|individual religious juridical persons|qid=Q11408858|1=Independent Shinto shrine}}|prefecture={{ill|Uji|qid=Q326956|1=Uji}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.889444444444|N|135.80777777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|qid=Q1370620|1=Fujiwara no Yorimichi}}; {{ill|Myōson|qid=Q11512055|1=Myōson}}|deity={{ill|Amitābha Buddha|qid=Q236242|1=Amitābha}}|part_of={{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|qid=Q874027|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}}}
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto Prefecture, Japan|qq=}} {{About||the arena in Debrecen, Hungary|Fonix Hall|the replica temple in Hawaii|Byodo-In Temple (Hawaii, USA)|qq=}} {{More citations needed|date= March 2008|qq=}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Byōdō-in | native_name = 平等院 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Phoenix Hall, Byodo-in, November 2016 -01.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = Phoenix Hall, a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 116 Ujirenge, {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|qid=Q326956|lt=Uji|1=Uji}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|22|N|135|48|28|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline, title|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = Independent | deity = [[Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Amitābha) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.byodoin.or.jp/|qq=}} | founded_by = {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|en|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|qid=Q1370620|lt=Fujiwara no Yorimichi|1=Fujiwara no Yorimichi}} | year_completed = 1052 }} {{nihongo|'''Byōdō-in'''|平等院|"Temple of Equality"|qq=}} is a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} temple in the city of {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|qid=Q326956|lt=Uji|1=Uji}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, built in the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref>Asian Historical Architecture: [http://www.orientalarchitecture.com/japan/kyoto/byodoin.php Byōdō-in.]</ref> It is jointly a temple of the [[Jōdo-shū]] (Pure Land) and {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai-shū|1=Tendai}} sects.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.byodoin.or.jp/en/faq.html#q1 |title=Byōdō-in official web site. |access-date=2016-03-26 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160408153136/http://www.byodoin.or.jp/en/faq.html#q1 |archive-date=2016-04-08 |url-status = dead|qq=}}</ref> == History == [[File:Byodo-in in Uji.jpg|thumb|left|250px|''Jōdo-shiki'' garden]] [[File:Byodoin Amitaabha Buddha.JPG|thumb|[[Amitābha]] in Byōdō-in created by {{ill|Jōchō|en|Jōchō|qid=Q60911|lt=Jōchō|1=Jōchō}}. 1053.]] This temple was originally built in 998 in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} as a rural villa of high-Ranking courtier {{ill|Minamoto no Shigenobu|pt|Minamoto no Shigenobu|ja|源重信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Shigenobu|1=Minamoto no Shigenobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11564321}}, Minister of the Left. After he died, one of the most powerful members of the [[Fujiwara clan]], {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|qid=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}}, purchased the property from the courtier's widow. The villa was made into a Buddhist temple by his son {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|en|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|qid=Q1370620|lt=Fujiwara no Yorimichi|1=Fujiwara no Yorimichi}} in 1052. Being one of the World Heritage sites of Japan, the Byodoin Temple buildings and Buddha statues have a long history of about 1000 years. In East Asian Buddhism, there is the [[:en:Three Ages of Buddhism|Three Ages of Buddhism]], which are three divisions of time following Buddha's passing. The Mappo, which is also translated as the Age of Dharma Decline, is the degenerate Third Age of Buddhism, also known as the Latter Day of the Law. It was widely believed that the year 1052 marked the first year of the beginning of the end-of-the-world. This theory captured the heart of many aristocrats and monks, which as a result, people became more devout in Buddhism and believed in the ideology of [[:en:Pure land|Buddhist Pure Land]]. In the following year, Amida-do Hall (Phoenix Hall) was completed in whose interior sits the 2.4 meters tall Amida Buddha statue created by {{ill|Jōchō|en|Jōchō|qid=Q60911|lt=Jōchō|1=Jōchō}}, who is claimed to have been the best Buddhist sculptor in the Heian Period. [[:en:Masashige Kusunoki|Masashige Kusunoki]], a samurai warrior who was fighting against the army of the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|qid=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga Clan|1=Ashikaga shogunate}}, set a fire around Byodoin Temple in 1336, which resulted in many of the temple buildings were burnt down with only a few surviving. The Byodoin Temple gradually expanded into a massive Temple complex during the [[:en:Kamakura Period|Kamakura Period]], where Jodo-in Temple was opened due to the renovation of Byodoin Temple in 1496; Rakan-do Hall was established in 1640; Saisho-in Temple was established in 1654. A full-scale renovation of the Amida-do Hall (Phoenix Hall) was undertaken in 1670, during which the front doors were replaced. Two sub-temples, Jodo-in Temple belonging to the Jodo Sect and Saisho-in Temple belonging to the Tendai Sect, began to cooperate and made a great effort to maintain the operation of Byodoin Temple. In 1698, Uji was devastated by a large fire and Byodoin Temple was badly damaged. The walls and doors of Phoenix Hall were ruined after this incident. However, as a result of the deterioration of the temple, people's will for Phoenix Hall's preservation and renovation became enthusiastic. During the [[:en:Meiji Period|Meiji Period]] (1868–1912) and [[:en:Showa Period|Showa Period]] (1926–1989), large scale renovation of the Phoenix hall was undertaken. The Phoenix Hall and Amida Buddha statue were designated as {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} in 1951, which Phoenix Hall was also selected for the design as the obverse of the {{ill|10 yen coin|en|10 yen coin|qid=Q4546963|lt=10 yen coin|1=10 yen coin}}. Then, the Temple Bell, the praying Bodhisattva on Clouds, the Canopy, the paintings on the door and walls of Cho-do Hall of Phoenix Hall were designated as National Treasures. The Byodoin Temple was designated as a [[:en:UNESCO World Heritage|UNESCO World Heritage]] as one of the Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto in 1994. Large scale renovation on the garden, the canopy of Amida Buddha statue and the overall outlook of the Phoenix Hall continues in the Heisei Period (1989–2019) until today. The most famous building in the temple is the {{nihongo|Phoenix Hall|鳳凰堂|Hōō-dō|qq=}} or the [[Amitābha|Amida]] Hall, constructed in 1053. It is the only remaining original building, surrounded by a scenic pond; additional buildings making up the compound were burnt down during a civil war in 1336. == Complex == === Phoenix Hall === [[File:JuEnDamaByodoinWP.png|right|thumb|Japanese {{ill|10 yen coin|en|10 yen coin|qid=Q4546963|lt=10 yen coin|1=10 yen coin}} (obverse) showing Phoenix Hall]] The main building in Byōdō-in, the Phoenix Hall consists of a central hall, flanked by twin wing corridors on both sides of the central hall, and a tail corridor. The central hall houses an image of Amida Buddha. The roof of the hall displays statues of {{ill|Fenghuang|en|Fenghuang|qid=Q527525|lt=the Chinese phoenix|1=Fenghuang}}, called ''hōō'' in Japanese. The architecture was influenced by the Chinese style (popular in the capital).<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Richie |first1=Donald |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ&dq=byodoin+kyoto+china&pg=PT80 |title=Temples of Kyoto |last2=Georges |first2=Alexandre |date=2012-08-07 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0857-8 |language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Elverskog |first=Johan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=N7_4Gr9Q438C&dq=byodoin++chinese&pg=PA85 |title=Buddhism and Islam on the Silk Road |date=2011-06-06 |publisher=University of Pennsylvania Press |isbn=978-0-8122-0531-2 |language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Daniell |first=Thomas |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UTjRAgAAQBAJ&dq=byodoin+tale+of+genji&pg=PT94 |title=Houses and Gardens of Kyoto: Revised with a new foreword by Matthew Stavros |date=2012-03-13 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-0590-4 |language=en|qq=}}</ref> The building is regarded as almost the sole remaining example from the [[:en:Fujiwara regent|Fujiwara Regent Period]], which is also considered as one of the most important cultural assets of Japan. The Phoenix Hall, completed in 1053, is the exemplar of Fujiwara Amida halls. It consists of a main rectangular structure flanked by two L-shaped wing corridors and a tail corridor, set at the edge of a large artificial pond. Though its official name is Amida-dō, it began to be called Hōō-dō, or Phoenix Hall, in the beginning of the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. This name is considered to derive both from the building's likeness to a chinese phoenix with outstretched wings and a tail, and the pair of phoenixes adorning the roof. Inside the Phoenix Hall, a single image of Amida (c. 1053) is installed on a high platform. The Amida sculpture is made of Japanese cypress and is covered with gold leaf. It was executed by {{ill|Jōchō|en|Jōchō|qid=Q60911|lt=Jōchō|1=Jōchō}}, who established a new [[:en:canon of proportions|canon of proportions]] and a new technique, ''{{ill|Yosegi|en|Yosegi|qid=Q15894888|lt=Yosegi|1=Yosegi-zaiku}}'', in which multiple pieces of wood are carved out like shells and joined from the inside. The statue measures about three meters high from its face to its knees. Sitting in a meditation position with his back slightly hunched, his body is relaxed without any tension. His hand gesture is called 'Inso', which both hands are placed on the lap, palms facing upward, thumbs and forefingers form a circle. This gesture is a variation of the [[:en:Dhyāna in Buddhism|Dhyana Mudra]] (Meditation Mudra), which suggests concentration of the Good Law. The {{ill|Urna|en|Urna|qid=Q261409|lt=Urna|1=Urna}}, which is the white dot on his forehead between the eyes, is the Third Eye of the Amida Buddha, a symbol of awakening and the ability to see the suffering of all creatures. The serene smile of the statue represents the nobility of the Buddha after attaining {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|qid=Q1137019|lt=Enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}. With a harmonious and gentle facial expression, he is looking slightly downward, which shows the state of meditation, as well as the feeling of merciful grace towards the audience. His hair is short and curled, along with Ushnisha, which is the pointed topknot located on the center of the head. Applied to the walls of the hall are small relief carvings of celestials, the host believed to have accompanied Amida when he descended from the Western Paradise to gather the souls of believers at the moment of death and transport them in lotus blossoms to Paradise. ''{{ill|Raigō|en|Raigō|qid=Q2019420|lt=Raigō|1=Raigō}}'' paintings on the wooden doors of the Phoenix Hall, depicting the Descent of the Amida Buddha, are an early example of {{ill|Yamato-e|en|Yamato-e|qid=Q597365|lt=Yamato-e|1=Yamato-e}}, Japanese-style painting, and contain representations of the scenery around Kyoto. There is a ''Jōdo-shiki'' garden with a pond in front of the building, which in 1997 was dredged as part of an archeological dig. As the temple faces the East, it suggests that the audience need to cross the Aji-ike pond in front of the Phoenix Hall, in order to reach Buddhist Pure Land. The gardens are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q858308|lt=nationally designated|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1642 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191223140731/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1642 |url-status = dead|archive-date=23 December 2019 |title=平等院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 |qq=}}</ref> === Kannon-do Hall === The Kannon-do Hall is an Important Cultural Property, established on the former site of the main hall around 1230, during the Early Kamakura Period. The hall structure is simple, which follows the classical design from the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|qid=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyo period|1=Tenpyō}} (710-794), having two different form bent to support the roof. It is currently under renovation and not open to the public. Japan commemorates the building's longevity and cultural significance by displaying its image on the {{ill|10 yen coin|en|10 yen coin|qid=Q4546963|lt=10 yen coin|1=10 yen coin}},<ref>Kyoto Travel: [http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3923.html Byōdō-in.]</ref> and the [[Banknotes of the Japanese yen#2004|10,000 yen note]] features the phoenix image. In December 1994, {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|qid=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} listed the building as a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}} as part of the "{{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|qid=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}". The Phoenix Hall, the great statue of Amida inside it, and several other items at Byōdō-in are {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=national treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. A {{ill|Byodo-In (Hawaii)|en|Byodo-In (Hawaii)|qid=Q5004179|lt=half-size replica|1=Byodo-In (Hawaii)}} of the temple was completed on June 7, 1968 in the {{ill|Valley of the Temples Memorial Park|en|Valley of the Temples Memorial Park|qid=Q7912373|lt=Valley of the Temples|1=Valley of the Temples Memorial Park}} ([[:en:O'ahu|O'ahu]], {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|qid=Q782|lt=Hawaii|1=Hawaii}}). The Japanese post has issued three definitive postage stamps showing the phoenix hall, each prepaying the then postal rate for a surface mail foreign letter: [[:commons:File:Byoudouin_24Yen_stamp_in_1950.JPG|1950]], 24 yen 1957 and 30 yen 1959. Stamps were produced by the costly engraving method, showing the appreciation of the hall. === Yorin-an Shoin Residence === It is a residence as well as one of the sub-temples in the precinct. Built in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] (around 17th century), the roof is thatched with [[:en:Japanese cypress|Japanese cypress]] bark, which the materials used for construction were believed came from remains of the [[:en:Momoyama Castle|Momoyama Castle]]. The interior wall paintings were the works produced by artists who belonged to the studio run by Sansetsu Kano, while the garden is said to have been designed by Sansai Hosokawa.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.byodoin.or.jp/en/around/watch/|title=Watch | Walk Around | World Heritage Byodoin|website=www.byodoin.or.jp|qq=}}</ref> === Hoshokan Museum === As the former temple museum which opened in 1965 had become outdated, an innovative third-generation museum was opened on March 1, 2001, which is named the Hoshokan Museum. This museum achieved a significantly improved storage and display environment for national treasures from the Byodoin Temple, including the Temple Bell, 26 statues of the Praying Bodhisattva on Clouds and a pair of Phoenix from the rooftop of Phoenix Hall. It is the first comprehensive museum run by a religious organisation. The majority of the museum buildings are hi11en underground, so that the exterior outlook of museum's blends with Phoenix Hall and other building complexes within the temple. Due to its design, the interior of the museum receives sufficient natural sunlight, as well as artificial lighting to keep the exhibition halls bright and comfortable for audience's experience.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.byodoin.or.jp/en/museum/|title=Hoshokan Museum | World Heritage Byodoin|website=www.byodoin.or.jp|qq=}}</ref> Entry to the complex grounds costs 700 yen for adults, and includes access to the gardens and the museum. An entry pass to the Phoenix Hall, which was newly restored in March 2014, costs an additional 300 yen and can be purchased near the gate. The tour is conducted in Japanese, however English paper guide book is provided. === Byodoin Tea Salon Toka === A tea salon to try authentic Uji green tea in the precinct of Byodoin Temple. Tea leaves harvested in the tea fields of Uji City or neighbouring farms are used. Certified Japanese Tea Instructors will provide tea to visitors with the finest care and knowledge. Open Monday to Sunday but closed on Tuesday, from 10:00 to 16:30. Last order is at 16:00.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.byodoin.or.jp/en/touka/|title=Byodoin Temple Tea Room "Sabo Toka" | World Heritage Byodoin|website=www.byodoin.or.jp|qq=}}</ref> == Gallery == <gallery> File:Byodoin-PhoenixHall-M1264.jpg|The head of the Jōchō statue of [[Amitābha]] is visible in this photo of the Phoenix Hall. File:Byodo-in Uji03bs2640.jpg|Left side of Phoenix Hall File:Byodo-in Uji04bs2640.jpg|The garden in front of the Phoenix Hall. File:Byodoin Uji Kyoto07s3s4592.jpg|Kannondo File:Byodoin Uji Kyoto09n4380.jpg|Omotemon, the main gate. File:Byodoin-tourgroupwalking-2015-12-29.webm|(video) Tourists walking into the Phoenix Hall on a sunny day. File:Byōdō-in Phoenix-Statue 09.2014.jpg|Byōdō-in Phoenix-Statue </gallery> == See also == {{Portal|Japan|Religion|Architecture|qq=}} *{{ill|Battle of Uji (1180)|en|Battle of Uji (1180)|qid=Q1887494|lt=Battle of Uji (1180)|1=Battle of Uji (1180)}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|qid=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} *{{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|qid=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|qid=Q3245521|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|qid=Q3245523|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} *{{ill|Shinden-zukuri|en|Shinden-zukuri|qid=Q2975225|lt=Shinden-zukuri|1=Shinden-zukuri}} == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == External links == {{Commons Category|Byōdō-in|qq=}} * [http://www.byodoin.or.jp/ Official homepage (in Japanese)] * [http://www.byodoin.or.jp/en/index.html Official homepage (in English)] * [http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/location/spot/shritemp/byodoin.html Japan National Tourism Organization: Byodo-in Temple] *[https://www.worldhistory.org/Byodo-in/ Byodo-in - World History Encyclopedia] {{World Heritage Kyoto|qq=}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan|qq=}} {{Authority control|qq=}}

Бьодо]] [[vi:Byōdō-in]] [[zh:平等院]] [[zh-yue:平等院]] {{wikidata link|Q61094}} [[Category:1052 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:10th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:10th-century establishments in
